a:r.a:t: Maturity assessment of foundational barriers across governance structure
Criteria
Observations
Leading Practice
Grade
Decision Ownership
Decision authority for compensation changes varies by region, function, and leadership group
Limited enterprise compensation philosophy and guiding principles
Regional autonomy often outweighs enterprise guardrails
Define an enterprise compensation philosophy and clear design guardrails
Establish a cross-functional governance forum spanning Sales, Marketing, Telemarketing, HR, Finance, and Sales Ops
Set decision rights by change type (annual plan changes, in-year changes, urgent exceptions)
Distinguish enterprise standards from approved site / channel variation
Define quorum, approval thresholds, and delegated authority levels
Maintain a formal decision log for key plan and policy decisions
Role Clarity (RACI)
Governance structure is not centralized across sales, marketing, and telemarketing
Finance, HR, and Sales leadership involvement is not consistently structured
Responsibilities across design, approvals, and administration are fragmented
Publish one end-to-end RACI across plan design, target setting, approvals, release, disputes, and reporting
Clarify who is accountable, who approves, who executes, and who provides input
Define data owners for key compensation inputs and outputs
Document handoffs, expected turnaround times, and control checkpoints
Assign backup / delegate roles to reduce bottlenecks
Refresh the RACI when organizational or process changes occur
Cross-Functional Alignment
Finance, HR, and Sales leadership involvement in compensation decisions is not consistently structured
Incentive decisions are not always tied to enterprise strategy
Establish recurring alignment checkpoints across Sales, Marketing, Telemarketing, HR, Finance, and Operations
Use a standard decision pack for plan changes, including business, financial, operational, and data impacts
Align on common KPI definitions, assumptions, and terminology before approvals
Review downstream process and reporting implications before final sign-off
Maintain a shared action / decision tracker across stakeholder groups
Escalate unresolved tradeoffs through a formal governance path
Dispute Management
Compensation decisions are often influenced by regional autonomy rather than enterprise guardrails
Dispute handling appears informal and varies by scenario or site
Manual adjustments increase rework and inconsistency
Define a standard exception taxonomy (e.g., manual adjustments, retro changes, disputes, payout holds, off-cycle requests)
Set approval thresholds and required evidence by exception type
Establish response-time SLAs and escalation paths for exceptions
Maintain a centralized exception log with rationale, approver, and resolution status
Publish playbooks for common edge cases and non-standard scenarios
Review exception volume regularly to identify recurring policy or process gaps
Limited Issues / Minor Opportunities for Improvement
Moderate Issues / Clear Opportunity for Improvement
Significant Issues Discovered / Action Required to Address Gaps
Compensation Governance Assessment
Governance Structure
Planning & Process
Tools & Data
